title: Negation in Uruangnirin
introduction:
How do languages say “not”? Across languages, three common patterns show up: (1) a special negative word (like “not” in “This is not a dog”), (2) a change on a word (an affix), like “im-” in “im-possible,” and (3) a special negative verb (for example, in Proto‑Tahitian). English mainly uses a special negative word, as in “This is not a dog,” and sometimes uses affixes like “im-.”
In Uruangnirin, the most frequent pattern is a special negative word too: the particle **tai**. Unlike English “not,” **tai** typically comes after the main part of the sentence (the predicate) and before clause‑final particles like **som** and **efi**. **tai** can also stand alone as “No.” Extra little words add time feelings: **efi** adds “yet,” so **tai efi** means “not yet,” and with the “ever” marker **boit**, it means “never” (“not yet ever”). **som** adds the “anymore/no longer” feeling. For saying that something doesn’t exist anymore, Uruangnirin also uses a special verb **tibyabin** “cease to exist/be gone,” often with **som**.

example.description: **tai** is used alone as a full negative reply (like saying “No”).

example.target_sentence: yo, lau kamkau tai som, lau tiperperi som
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: After an equative predicate, **tai** negates inclusion in a category; **som** adds the “anymore/no longer” nuance. The order is predicate + **tai** + **som**.

example.target_sentence: sekarang anunggi nenang akanata lau kuat tai som
example.source_sentence: Today my mother would not be able to carry me on her back!
example.description: An adjectival/modal predicate is negated with **tai**; **som** contributes the “(anymore/no longer)” nuance. Placement: predicate + **tai** + **som**.

example.target_sentence: o boit fika ia tai efi
example.description: **tai efi** means “not yet.” With **boit** (“ever/experiential”), it yields “never” (“not yet ever”). **tai** follows the predicate and precedes **efi**.

example.target_sentence: lau boit ifika sinina foto tai efi
example.source_sentence: Well, I’ve met some of your relatives, but I’ve never seen your pictures.
example.description: Same pattern: experiential **boit** + predicate + **tai efi** → “never.” **tai** appears after the predicate and before **efi**.

sections.description: Uruangnirin mostly negates with the special particle **tai**. It can negate different kinds of predicates (verbs, equatives, adjectives/modals) and even stand alone as “No.” Typical placement is: predicate + **tai**, then clause‑final items like **som** or **efi**.

sections.focus: Time shades with negation: **tai efi** “not yet,” and **boit … tai efi** “never”

example.target_sentence: o boit fika ia tai efi
lau boit ifika sinina foto tai efi
example.source_sentence: Well, I’ve met some of your relatives, but I’ve never seen your pictures.

example.target_sentence: sinanua amata asusi lau tai efi
example.source_sentence: They died before I was born.
example.description: “Not yet” with **tai efi** anchored to the event of being born. It expresses that, at that time, I did not yet exist (so the event happened before my birth).

sections.description: **efi** adds the idea of “yet.” **tai efi** is “not yet.” When you add the experiential marker **boit** (“ever”), the combo **boit** + predicate + **tai efi** gives “never” (“not yet ever”). **tai** still comes after the predicate and before **efi**.

example.target_sentence: yo, lau kamkau tai som, lau tiperperi som
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: With ordinary predicates, **som** with **tai** contributes “anymore/no longer”: predicate + **tai som**. The second clause shows **som** as a general clause‑final particle (without **tai**).

example.target_sentence: sekarang anunggi nenang akanata lau kuat tai som
example.source_sentence: Today my mother would not be able to carry me on her back!
example.description: Modal/quality negation with **tai**; **som** adds the “no longer/anymore” nuance.

example.target_sentence: amin sekola tuaruari, ia tibyabin som
example.source_sentence: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.
example.description: Existential negation uses the verb **tibyabin** “cease to exist/be gone,” often followed by **som** to mean “anymore/no longer.”

sections.description: To say something is not the case anymore, Uruangnirin uses **som**. With regular predicates, **tai … som** gives “not … anymore/no longer.” For existence, Uruangnirin uses the special verb **tibyabin** “be gone,” commonly with **som** → “doesn’t exist anymore.”

sections.focus: What kinds of negation we see (and don’t see) in the data

example.target_sentence: o boit fika ia tai efi
example.description: Process negation: the action never happened. Pattern: **boit** + predicate + **tai efi**.

example.target_sentence: yo, lau kamkau tai som, lau tiperperi som
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: Negation of inclusion in a category: equative predicate + **tai**; **som** adds “anymore/no longer.”

example.target_sentence: sekarang anunggi nenang akanata lau kuat tai som
example.source_sentence: Today my mother would not be able to carry me on her back!
example.description: Negation of a quality/modality with **tai**, plus **som** for the “anymore/no longer” nuance.

example.target_sentence: amin sekola tuaruari, ia tibyabin som
example.source_sentence: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.
example.description: Existential negation with **tibyabin**, often with **som** → “no longer exists.”

example.target_sentence: sinanua amata asusi lau tai efi
example.source_sentence: They died before I was born.
example.description: “Not yet (exist)” via **tai efi** anchored to birth → a “before” meaning.

sections.description: Attested sentence‑level negation types in our data: processes (including “never”), inclusion in a category, qualities/modal meanings, and existence. Not shown in the data: constituent‑internal negation (like “non‑X”), negation of location, and negation of identification with a specific entity. We also see no negative affixes; the core tool is the particle **tai**, with a special existential verb **tibyabin**. For possession, we do not have examples that contrast “I have no X” (existence) vs. “This X is not mine” (relationship).

conclusion: Remember: Uruangnirin’s main negation tool is the particle **tai**, placed after the predicate and before clause‑final pieces like **som** and **efi**. Use **tai efi** for “not yet,” and with **boit** it becomes “never.” Use **som** to add “anymore/no longer,” and use the verb **tibyabin** for “doesn’t exist anymore.” Some types (like location negation, identification, or inside‑noun “non‑X”) aren’t shown in our data.

translation_drills.source: Well, I’ve met some of your relatives, but I’ve never seen your pictures.

yo, lau kamkau tai som, lau tiperperi som

translation_drills.source: Today my mother would not be able to carry me on her back!

translation_drills.source: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.

translation_drills.source: They died before I was born.
